title: Deep Generative Models for Bayesian Inference in Astrophysics
speaker: Biwei Dai
abstract:
Deep generative models offer powerful tools for solving astrophysical inference problems by enabling flexible representations of prior knowledge and likelihood functions. 
In the first part of the talk, I will discuss how generative models can be employed to construct likelihood functions for cosmological inference at the field level, enabling more effective extraction of information compared to traditional summary statistics like two-point statistics. This simulation-based inference framework facilitates anomaly detection of model misspecification, and enhances interpretability through sample generation. I will present applications to weak gravitational lensing analysis, particularly our ongoing work on applying this approach to the field-level analysis of the Hyper Suprime-Cam (HSC) survey.
In the second part, I will demonstrate how generative models can be used to construct physically informed priors for Bayesian inverse problems. As an application, I will show how this approach enables image reconstruction of AGN accretion disks from intensity interferometry, where only the amplitudes of Fourier modes are measured while phase information is lost. By sampling from the resulting posterior distribution, we achieve high-fidelity reconstructions with uncertainty quantification, outperforming traditional iterative methods across varying noise levels and UV-plane coverage.
tags: Institute for Advanced Study Astrophysics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Bayesian inverse-problem formulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames many astrophysical observations as indirect inverse problems and argues that Bayesian inference is natural but often lacks tractable analytic priors or likelihoods.

proof_stages.label: Generative models as learned probability laws
proof_stages.summary: Generative models, especially normalizing flows and diffusion models, are introduced as high-dimensional probability distributions that can learn priors or likelihoods from simulations and then be inserted into Bayes' theorem.

proof_stages.label: Field-level weak-lensing likelihood learning
proof_stages.summary: For weak-lensing cosmology, simulations at given cosmological parameters are used to train a conditional likelihood for full map pixels, yielding tighter field-level parameter constraints than power spectra or other compressed summaries.

proof_stages.label: Likelihood-based reliability diagnostics
proof_stages.summary: Because the generative model provides likelihood values, the speaker develops goodness-of-fit, out-of-distribution, ROC-based, and prospective Bayes-factor diagnostics to detect simulation-observation mismatch and systematic errors.

proof_stages.label: Scale diagnostics and map-level interpretability
proof_stages.summary: Wavelet decompositions compare posteriors from different scales to identify contaminated scales, while sampling and latent-code manipulations in the flow visualize which structures drive cosmological exclusions.

proof_stages.label: HSC weak-lensing deployment
proof_stages.summary: The weak-lensing framework is being applied to HSC data using high-resolution ray-tracing simulations, systematic modeling, scale cuts, baryonic marginalization, and preliminary likelihood checks that reveal possible low-redshift/small-scale mismatch.

proof_stages.label: Bayesian phase retrieval with learned image priors
proof_stages.summary: For intensity interferometry, where only Fourier-amplitude information is observed, the reconstruction problem is recast as Bayesian phase retrieval with a measurement-noise likelihood and a generative prior learned from simulated images.

proof_stages.label: Misspecified-prior robustness for image reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: The image-reconstruction method is stress-tested under mismatched priors, showing how high signal-to-noise data can overcome prior structure while weak data revert toward the prior, and noting that the same likelihood diagnostics can test prior-data agreement.

examples.note: A synthetic scale-dependent noise error shows how the multiscale posterior comparison flags biased small-scale information and recovers unbiased constraints after removing it.
